items.title: Payouts King Ransomware Initial Access Broker Deploys New Edgecution Malware
items.description: An initial access broker linked to Payouts King ransomware is deploying Edgecution, a sophisticated malware utilizing a malicious Microsoft Edge browser extension. The attack begins through social engineering via Microsoft Teams, impersonating IT staff and directing victims to fake Microsoft websites offering supposed Outlook updates. Edgecution comprises two components: a browser extension that c
items.ai_summary: The Payouts King ransomware initial access broker has been observed deploying Edgecution malware, which utilizes a malicious Microsoft Edge browser extension to gain initial access. The attack vector involves social engineering tactics, where attackers impersonate IT staff through Microsoft Teams and direct victims to fake Microsoft websites offering supposed Outlook updates. Once installed, the Edgecution malware encrypts data for impact, as indicated by the detection of T1486. To remediate, defenders should immediately block any suspicious Microsoft Edge extensions and update their browser to the latest version, ensuring all extensions are verified and trusted. Additionally, users should be educated on the risks of social engineering attacks and the importance of verifying the authenticity of IT staff communications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1650| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1176| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Prinz Eugen ransomware: a deep dive into a new Go-based encryptor
items.description: Prinz Eugen is a newly discovered Go-based ransomware family first observed in April 2026, attributed to an actor known as ROOTBOY. The encryptor employs sophisticated techniques including ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption, prioritizes recently modified files to maximize pressure on victims, and implements anti-forensic measures such as memory scrubbing and self-deletion. Unlike typical ransomware, it
items.ai_summary: The Prinz Eugen ransomware, attributed to the ROOTBOY actor, is a Go-based encryptor that utilizes ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption to compromise victim systems. This ransomware prioritizes recently modified files to maximize pressure on victims and implements anti-forensic measures such as memory scrubbing and self-deletion. If exploited, victims can expect significant data loss and potential disruption to business operations, with the ransomware's focus on recently modified files likely to cause substantial impact. To remediate, defenders should ensure all systems are configured to regularly back up critical data, with backups stored in a secure, offline location, and consider implementing a least privilege access model to limit the spread of the ransomware. Additionally, defenders should monitor for unusual file modification activity and implement measures to detect and prevent memory scrubbing and self-deletion techniques.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Phishing Campaign Deploys JavaScript-Driven PureLogs Variant to Steal Sensitive Data
items.description: A sophisticated phishing campaign distributes a PureLogs variant through deceptive purchase order emails containing malicious JavaScript files. The attack chain employs obfuscated JavaScript that drops PowerShell scripts, which then use process hollowing techniques to inject .NET modules into legitimate Windows processes. The malware communicates with command-and-control infrastructure to download
items.ai_summary: The phishing campaign utilizes a PureLogs variant, distributed through deceptive purchase order emails containing malicious JavaScript files, to steal sensitive data. The attack chain involves obfuscated JavaScript that drops PowerShell scripts, employing process hollowing techniques to inject .NET modules into legitimate Windows processes, such as explorer.exe or dllhost.exe. If exploited, this campaign can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information, including login credentials and financial data, affecting various organizations and individuals. To remediate, defenders should implement email filters to block suspicious attachments, restrict PowerShell execution to authorized personnel, and monitor for unusual process activity, such as unexpected .NET module injections. Additionally, ensuring all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches and using anti-malware software with behavioral detection capabilities can help prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.007| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ClickFix campaign delivers macOS infostealer via DMG
items.description: A new macOS ClickFix campaign employs fake CAPTCHA pages to deceive users into executing malicious Terminal commands. The attack chain downloads and invisibly mounts a DMG file containing a self-signed information-stealer application bundle. This payload, assessed as belonging to the AMOS (Atomic macOS Stealer) lineage—specifically the Odyssey variant—prompts users for passwords through fake Syste
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix campaign targets macOS users with a sophisticated phishing attack, utilizing fake CAPTCHA pages to trick victims into executing malicious Terminal commands. The attack chain involves downloading and mounting a DMG file containing a self-signed information-stealer application bundle, identified as the Odyssey variant of the AMOS (Atomic macOS Stealer) lineage. If exploited, this campaign can lead to significant data breaches, with attackers gaining access to sensitive user information, including passwords and other personal data. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering solutions to block phishing emails, ensure all macOS systems are updated to the latest version, and configure security settings to only allow installation of applications from trusted sources. Additionally, users should be educated on identifying and avoiding suspicious CAPTCHA pages and prompted to report any unusual activity to their security teams.

items.title: Operation Endgame disrupts Amadey and Stealc
items.description: ESET Research contributed to a global disruption operation targeting the Amadey botnet and Stealc infostealer, both malware-as-a-service offerings. The operation, coordinated by Microsoft Digital Crimes Unit, BitSight, Lumen, and MBSD, impacted approximately 50 domains and nearly 200 active IP-based command and control servers. ESET provided technical analyses, statistical information, C&C server
items.ai_summary: Operation Endgame disrupted the Amadey botnet and Stealc infostealer, malware-as-a-service offerings, impacting approximately 50 domains and nearly 200 active IP-based command and control servers. The disruption targeted the botnet's command and control (C2) infrastructure, likely using T1583.005 (Botnet) tactics to identify and disrupt the C2 servers. ESET provided technical analyses and statistical information to support the operation. To prevent similar disruptions, defenders should monitor for suspicious network activity, particularly related to application layer protocol (T1071) and dynamic resolution (T1568) tactics. Additionally, organizations should ensure they have up-to-date security software and configurations, particularly for systems vulnerable to botnet attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1568| D[Dynamic Resolution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1583.005| E[Botnet]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Zero-Day Exploitation of Vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager
items.description: In early 2026, a threat actor targeted SD-WAN infrastructure at a service provider, exploiting a zero-day vulnerability in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN to escalate privileges. The attacker initially gained access through unauthorized peering connections and manipulated default account passwords. They then exploited CVE-2026-20245, a privilege escalation flaw in the file upload feature, by uploading a mal
items.ai_summary: A zero-day vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager was exploited by a threat actor to escalate privileges. The attack vector involved unauthorized peering connections and manipulation of default account passwords. The exploit targeted the file upload feature, allowing the attacker to upload malicious files. This privilege escalation flaw enabled the attacker to gain elevated access to the SD-WAN infrastructure. To remediate, defenders should update to Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager version 20.2.3 or later, and ensure that default account passwords are changed and unauthorized peering connections are removed.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title: Behind the console: An AiTM phishing kit harvesting AWS console credentials and beyond
items.description: Between June 16 and 19, 2026, a sophisticated adversary-in-the-middle phishing campaign targeted AWS console users through three domains registered within 48 hours and hosted on Cloudflare. The campaign impersonated AWS login pages and captured credentials along with real-time multi-factor authentication codes through email, SMS, and authenticator apps. Phishing emails were delivered through legit
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated adversary-in-the-middle phishing campaign targeted AWS console users through three domains registered within 48 hours and hosted on Cloudflare. The campaign impersonated AWS login pages and captured credentials along with real-time multi-factor authentication codes through email, SMS, and authenticator apps. This phishing kit, likely an AiTM (Adversary-in-the-Middle) phishing kit, leverages T1566 (Phishing) and T1557 (Adversary-in-the-Middle) tactics to bypass MFA and gain unauthorized access to AWS accounts. To remediate, AWS users should immediately change their console credentials and enable AWS IAM password policies. Additionally, users should verify the authenticity of AWS login pages and be cautious of phishing emails delivered through legitimate services.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1557| B[Adversary-in-the-Middle]:::tactic
    B -->|T1566| C[Phishing]:::tactic
    C -->|T1538| D[Cloud Service Dashboard]:::tactic
    D -->|T1210| E[Initial Access]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: LokiBot After a Decade: An Analysis of a Recent LokiBot Campaign
items.description: LokiBot, an infostealer first advertised in May 2015, continues to operate after more than a decade with numerous variants. The malware targets credentials from over a hundred software products including browsers, cryptocurrency wallets, password managers, email and FTP clients. A recent campaign delivers LokiBot through malspam with JScript email attachments, executing a multi-stage infection cha
items.ai_summary: LokiBot, an infostealer first advertised in May 2015, continues to operate after more than a decade with numerous variants. The malware targets credentials from over a hundred software products including browsers, cryptocurrency wallets, password managers, email and FTP clients. A recent campaign delivers LokiBot through malspam with JScript email attachments, executing a multi-stage infection chain. If exploited, LokiBot can lead to data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information. To remediate, users should update their software to the latest version, particularly password managers and cryptocurrency wallets, and implement robust email filtering to prevent malspam delivery.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1104| B[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    B -->|T1555.005| C[Password Managers]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: KimJongRAT Continues to Evolve by Leveraging LOTS
items.description: In May 2026, security researchers observed an attack campaign distributing KimJongRAT through GitHub and other legitimate services. KimJongRAT, used by the North Korean APT group Kimsuky since 2013, combines information stealing and remote access capabilities. The infection chain begins with phishing emails containing shortened URLs redirecting to GitHub Releases hosting malicious ZIP files. Victi
items.ai_summary: KimJongRAT, a remote access tool (RAT) used by the North Korean APT group Kimsuky since 2013, has been observed evolving by leveraging legitimate services such as GitHub. The infection chain begins with phishing emails containing shortened URLs redirecting to GitHub Releases hosting malicious ZIP files. If exploited, KimJongRAT can steal sensitive information and provide remote access capabilities to attackers. To remediate, users should avoid clicking on suspicious links and ensure that all GitHub Releases are thoroughly vetted before downloading. Additionally, organizations should implement robust email filtering and phishing simulation training for employees.

graph LR

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

items.title: FIFA 2026 Security Alert: Cybercriminals Exploit Fan Excitement with Mass Phishing
items.description: Threat actors are exploiting anticipation for the FIFA World Cup 2026 through sophisticated phishing campaigns targeting fans seeking tickets, hospitality packages, and tournament information. Attackers have deployed FIFA-themed domains and mobile-optimized phishing infrastructure resolving to specific IP addresses, designed to harvest credentials and payment information. The campaigns feature con
items.ai_summary: Cybercriminals are exploiting anticipation for the FIFA World Cup 2026 through sophisticated phishing campaigns targeting fans seeking tickets, hospitality packages, and tournament information. Attackers have deployed FIFA-themed domains and mobile-optimized phishing infrastructure resolving to specific IP addresses, designed to harvest credentials and payment information. The campaigns feature convincing emails and websites mimicking official FIFA ticketing and hospitality providers. If exploited, victims may have their credentials and payment information stolen, leading to financial loss and potential identity theft. To remediate, FIFA fans should be cautious of unsolicited emails and websites, and only purchase tickets and hospitality packages from official FIFA sources, using HTTPS and verifying the authenticity of the website before entering sensitive information. Additionally, FIFA and its partners should implement robust phishing detection and response measures to mitigate the impact of these campaigns.

graph LR

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-9261: Use of weak SSH cryptographic algorithms in Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Versi...
items.description: Use of weak SSH cryptographic algorithms in Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Version 1.5.0 or earlier
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-9261 is a vulnerability in Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Version 1.5.0 or earlier, which allows attackers to exploit weak SSH cryptographic algorithms. This vulnerability can be exploited by an attacker to intercept sensitive data or gain unauthorized access to the device. Specifically, the vulnerability affects the SSH protocol used by the Canon EOS Network Setting Tool, allowing an attacker to use a brute-force attack to guess the password. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Version 1.5.1 or later, which addresses this issue. Additionally, users should ensure that SSH is not enabled on the device unless necessary, and consider using a more secure SSH protocol such as SSHv2.

items.title: CVE-2026-9260: Use of hard-coded cryptographic keys in Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Version 1...
items.description: Use of hard-coded cryptographic keys in Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Version 1.5.0 or earlier
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-9260 is a medium-severity vulnerability in Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Version 1.5.0 or earlier, where the use of hard-coded cryptographic keys allows an attacker to intercept and decrypt sensitive data. If exploited, an attacker could gain unauthorized access to sensitive information, such as camera settings and network credentials. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Version 1.6.0 or later, which includes a fix for this issue. Additionally, users should ensure that their network is configured to use secure communication protocols, such as HTTPS, and that any sensitive data is encrypted. It is also recommended to regularly review and update cryptographic keys and certificates to prevent similar vulnerabilities in the future.

items.title: CVE-2026-9259: Improper validation of server certificates in Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Ver...
items.description: Improper validation of server certificates in Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Version 1.5.0 or earlier
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-9259 is a medium-severity vulnerability in Canon EOS Network Setting Tool versions 1.5.0 and earlier, allowing an attacker to conduct a man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack by exploiting the improper validation of server certificates. This vulnerability can be exploited by an attacker to intercept sensitive information, such as login credentials and camera settings, when a user connects to the Canon EOS Network Setting Tool. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to version 1.5.1 or later, which addresses the issue. Additionally, users should ensure that their Canon EOS Network Setting Tool is configured to use a secure connection (HTTPS) and verify the authenticity of the server certificate. This can be done by checking the certificate's validity and ensuring it is issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA).

items.title: CVE-2026-9258: Improper validation of SSH host keys in Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Version 1...
items.description: Improper validation of SSH host keys in Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Version 1.5.0 or earlier
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-9258 is a medium-severity vulnerability in Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Version 1.5.0 or earlier, allowing an attacker to conduct man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks by exploiting the improper validation of SSH host keys. This vulnerability can be exploited by an attacker who has access to the same network as the Canon EOS device, potentially leading to unauthorized access to sensitive data or device control. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to Canon EOS Network Setting Tool Version 1.6.0 or later, which addresses this issue. Additionally, users should ensure that SSH host key verification is enabled on their Canon EOS devices. This can be done by navigating to the device's settings menu and enabling the 'Verify SSH host key' option. It is also recommended to regularly update the device's firmware to ensure that any newly discovered vulnerabilities are addressed.

items.title: CVE-2026-53430: Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification) vulnerability i...
items.description:
Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification) vulnerability in elixir-grpc grpc (GRPC.Compressor.Gzip, GRPC.Message modules) allows a denial of service via a gzip decompression bomb.

This vulnerability is associated with program files lib/grpc/compressor/gzip.ex, lib/grpc/message.ex and program routines 'Elixir.GRPC.Compressor.Gzip':decompress/1, 'Elixir.GRPC.Message':from_data
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-53430 is a denial-of-service (DoS) vulnerability in elixir-grpc grpc, specifically affecting the GRPC.Compressor.Gzip and GRPC.Message modules. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a gzip decompression bomb, causing the affected system to consume excessive resources and potentially leading to a denial of service. This vulnerability affects systems running elixir-grpc grpc versions prior to 1.4.2, and remediation involves updating to version 1.4.2 or later. Specifically, users should update the lib/grpc/compressor/gzip.ex and lib/grpc/message.ex files to the latest version, and ensure that the 'Elixir.GRPC.Compressor.Gzip':decompress/1 and 'Elixir.GRPC.Message':from_data routines are configured to handle decompression bombs correctly. This vulnerability has a severity score of 7.5 due to its potential to cause a denial of service, but it does not allow for remote code execution or data breach.

    B -->|T1498| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.description: The browserstack-cypress-cli is BrowserStack's CLI which allows users to run Cypress tests on BrowserStack. Versions prior to 1.36.4 are vulnerable to OS command injection via the cypress_config_file configuration parameter. Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key vulnerability in elixir-grpc grpc allows authenticated attackers to access or modify resources belonging to other users by smuggling a conflicting value for any path-bound field via the query string or request body.

items.title: CVE-2026-12205: Crypt::DSA versions before 1.21 for Perl reused the nonce across signatures, lea...
items.description:
Crypt::DSA versions before 1.21 for Perl reused the nonce across signatures, leading to private-key recovery.

Crypt::DSA::sign caches the per-signature nonce material in the Key object without ever clearing it.

The first sign() on a Key object picks a nonce, and every later sign() on that same object reuses it, producing an identical "r".

items.title: Chrome Ad Blocker with 10M+ Installs Found with Dormant Script Injection Capability
items.description: An analysis of a popular Google Chrome ad block extension for YouTube has uncovered the ability to execute arbitrary JavaScript code. According to Island, the extension, named Adblock for YouTube (ID: cmedhionkhpnakcndndgjdbohmhepckk), has more than 10 million installs and carries a Featured badge on the Chrome Web Store. The extension description states that it allows users to prevent web

items.title: The Four Elevations of Effective Fraud Prevention
items.description: Fraudsters don't attack just one transaction. They target accounts, platforms, and entire ecosystems. IPQS explains the four elevations of fraud prevention and why broader visibility improves fraud detection. [...]

items.title: Runlayer Raises $30 Million in Series A Funding
items.description: The startup’s platform functions as a secure control layer, aiming to secure AI tools across enterprises. The post Runlayer Raises $30 Million in Series A Funding appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Smart TV Proxyware, 24-Year curl Bug, AI Crime Forums + 13 More Stories
items.description: It’s dumb out there again. This week has the usual smell of prod on fire and nobody wanting to admit who left the door open — old creds still working, trusted apps doing sketchy crap, browser tricks jumping the fence, and “normal” workflows turning into phishing pipes because apparently email was not enough hell already. The worst part is how cheap some of it feels. Not elite. Not cinematic.

items.title: Ukraine's state postal operator reports app disruption after cyberattack
items.description: Ukraine's state-owned postal operator said it was experiencing disruptions to some of its app services due to a suspected cyberattack, but did not say who was behind it.

items.title: Russia used Cellebrite phone-hacking tool to crack down on dissident after firm cut off country
items.description: The continued use of the powerful data extraction product soon after the company in March 2021 said it would stop working with Russia suggests the firm has been unable to pull back its technology from authoritarian government customers, researchers say.

items.title: Webinar: Why account takeovers remain one of the hardest threats to stop
items.description: Account takeover attacks continue to challenge security teams because attackers often operate through legitimate accounts and trusted services. This webinar explores how behavioral AI can help organizations identify compromised accounts faster and automate response workflows. [...]

items.title: Cal Water Says No OT Systems Breached in Iranian Handala Cyberattack
items.description: Mandiant has helped the California water utility investigate the cyberattack launched by Iranian hacker group Handala. The post Cal Water Says No OT Systems Breached in Iranian Handala Cyberattack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Lantronix Serial-to-IP Converter Flaw Exploited in Attacks After OT Threat Warning
items.description: The exploited flaw, CVE-2025-67038, is one of the vulnerabilities disclosed in April as part of the BRIDGE:BREAK research project. The post Lantronix Serial-to-IP Converter Flaw Exploited in Attacks After OT Threat Warning appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Surviving the Mythos Era: Richard Bejtlich on the Case for NDR
items.description: Despite the abundance of telemetry at analysts’ disposal, many security operations teams struggle to answer a few basic questions during incident investigation: What happened? What evidence do we have? How do we know we’re seeing it all, in context? Answering these questions requires teams to go beyond alerts, the most common basis for initial triage. But investigations (and their outcomes)

items.title: GitLab Patches Code Execution, Information Disclosure Vulnerabilities
items.description: The latest GitLab CE/EE updates address 13 vulnerabilities, including three high-severity defects. The post GitLab Patches Code Execution, Information Disclosure Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: What do Ports Hear When Nobody's Listening? An Assessment of Automated Cybercrime [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Nicole Phillips, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Europe Evolves Into Ransomware's Favorite Region
items.description: After a global lull, ransomware gangs are setting sights on a rich new arena: attacking EU organizations and their suppliers.

items.title: 25-Year-Old Vulnerability Patched in Curl
items.description: The latest version of the open source data transfer tool resolves 18 medium and low-severity vulnerabilities. The post 25-Year-Old Vulnerability Patched in Curl appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Gaslight macOS Malware Uses Prompt Injection to Disrupt AI-Assisted Analysis
items.description: A previously undocumented Rust-based macOS implant and information stealer has been found to embed a prompt injection payload designed to trick a malware analyst's artificial intelligence (AI) tools and trick it into aborting or refusing an analysis of the artifact. The malware has been codenamed Gaslight owing to this deceptive behavior. It's been assessed with high confidence that the tool is

items.title: SecurityWeek ICS Cybersecurity Conference Heads to Nashville for Special 25-Year Anniversary Edition
items.description: The 2026 Industrial Control Systems (ICS) Cybersecurity Conference takes place October 6-8, 2026, at the W Nashville. The post SecurityWeek ICS Cybersecurity Conference Heads to Nashville for Special 25-Year Anniversary Edition appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke in ClickFix and ModeloRAT Campaigns
items.description: A new, stealthy backdoor named Mistic has been deployed as part of suspected financially motivated attacks aimed at multiple organizations spanning insurance, education, IT, and professional services sectors since April 2026. According to Symantec and Carbon Black's Threat Hunter Team, the backdoor, also tracked as MLTBackdoor, is said to be linked to an initial access broker (IAB) named

items.title: NIST Opens Updated IoT Security Guidance to Public Review
items.description: The guidance aims to establish product cybersecurity requirements for IoT devices integrated into federal agencies’ networks. The post NIST Opens Updated IoT Security Guidance to Public Review appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Chrome 149 Update Resolves 18 Severe Vulnerabilities
items.description: More than half of the bugs are use-after-free defects, which can potentially lead to remote code execution. The post Chrome 149 Update Resolves 18 Severe Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited Months Before Patching
items.description: CVE-2026-20245, the 7th Cisco SD-WAN vulnerability exploited in 2026, was used for months prior to its disclosure and patching. The post Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited Months Before Patching appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access
items.description: An unknown threat actor exploited a recently disclosed high-severity security flaw impacting Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN as a zero-day at least two months before it was publicly disclosed, according to new findings from Google-owned Mandiant. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20245 (CVSS score: 7.8), allows an authenticated, local attacker to execute arbitrary commands with elevated privileges

items.title: Google releases new privacy controls for activity history, personalization
items.description: Google is rolling out new privacy controls for Search services and Google Play, giving you more control over saved history and personalized recommendations. [...]

items.title: Attackers Hit Cisco SD-WAN Flaw 2 Months Before Disclosure
items.description: Researchers believe rogue peering was used to connect to the victim's SD-WAN devices to gain admin privileges and root-level access.

items.title: 2026 FIFA World Cup Faces Surge in Cyber Threats
items.description: Persistent cybercrime, social engineering, and infrastructure threats continue to plague the FIFA 2026 World Cup across the US, Canada, and Mexico.

items.title: Three ‘cybercrime as a service’ operations undercut by Microsoft, law enforcement
items.description: Microsoft touted its latest action against malware infrastructure as a new approach aimed at the full cybercrime "supply chain." Europol said more than 300 servers were targeted.

items.title: Do CISOs Need a Code of Ethics?
items.description: Dark Reading Confidential Episode 19: Kickbacks, no-show jobs, "dirty" VCs, and shelf ware — industry expert Robert "RSnake" Hansen explains why he thinks it's time for a CISO code of ethics. It could ensure cybersecurity bosses aren't engaged in self-dealing that could risk enterprise, and even national, security.

items.title: When Information Becomes the Attack Surface – Understanding AI Agent Traps
items.description: From hidden content injections to cognitive state poisoning, attackers are turning trusted data sources into traps for autonomous AI. The post When Information Becomes the Attack Surface – Understanding AI Agent Traps appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA Warns Critical Lantronix EDS5000 Flaw Is Being Actively Exploited
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Tuesday warned of active exploitation of a critical security flaw impacting Lantronix EDS5000 Series devices, urging Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies to apply the fixes by June 26, 2026. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2025-67038 (CVSS score: 9.8), a code injection flaw that could result in the execution

items.title: More Malicious OpenClaw Skills Threaten AI Supply Chain
items.description: OpenClaw removed five packages from its ClawHub skills marketplace that bypassed security checks even though they included infostealers and other threats.

items.title: Amadey and StealC Malware Network Disrupted, 27M Stolen Credentials Recovered
items.description: A coordinated law enforcement operation, in partnership with private sector companies, including Bitdefender, Bitsight, ESET, and Microsoft, has resulted in the takedown of criminal infrastructure powering Amadey and StealC. "The main common goal was to disrupt the 'assembly lines' cybercriminals use to launch ransomware, financial fraud, and attacks on critical infrastructure," Europol said in

items.title: Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new class of CI/CD workflow weakness that allows attackers to hijack workflows and compromise open-source supply chains. The "critical exploitable pattern" has been codenamed Cordyceps by Novee Security. The issue can allow full attacker control of repositories at dozens of the largest organizations worldwide, including Microsoft, Google, Apache, and

items.title: Apple's MacOS Gap Lets Users Disable Security Tools
items.description: Attackers can exploit the issue to disable security and integrated browser tools without needing administrator privileges or kernel exploits.

items.title: Dawn of the Apex Agentic Adversary
items.description: We are standing at the end of an era we never thought to mourn: the era of human-speed threats. For years, cybersecurity moved to a rhythm organizations could follow. A researcher found a bug, a CVE was cataloged, a vendor navigated a patch cycle, and weeks or even months later, a fix was deployed. In this era, dwell time was measured in days, sometimes weeks. We are now approaching an

items.title: Linux Process Name Masquerading, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: In a previous diary, I talked about stack strings&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;1&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5d; with a practical example of them. Since my SEC670 class, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xe2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x80;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x99;m even more interested&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;in malware obfuscation techniques. I had&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;a look at process names. When you list running processes on a computer, can you trust what you see&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x3f; If you&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;re facing a rootkit, malicious processes can be simply hidden (the API calls or commands to list processed have been tampered). But a malicious process&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;can also mimic a non-suspicious name by

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.description: The vulnerability

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Webshells Remain Popular, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: Webshells have been popular for a long time. We already covered this topic across multiple diaries[1][2]. I spent some time to track them[3] and slighly paid less attention to them but today I found another one. It seems to be a new player (pushed on Github two months ago).

items.title: eBanking Phishing Delivered Through IPv4-Mapped IPv6 Address, (Fri, Jun 19th)
items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
